questions.question: Explain the importance of Host OS security in cloud computing. Describe the key hardening strategies for securing the Host OS.
keyPoints: The Host OS has the highest privilege level after the hypervisor — its compromise allows access to all VMs, virtual disks, and memory on that physical host.
Minimal installation: only essential packages, remove GUI, compilers, dev tools to reduce attack surface.
Dedicated purpose: Host OS should only run hypervisor and management tools — no other services.
Mandatory access controls: SELinux (enforcing) or AppArmor to confine processes beyond DAC permissions.
Patch management: automated, regular patching with staging environment testing; live patching (kpatch) for zero-downtime.
Network hardening: management interface on isolated VLAN, host firewall, encrypted channels (SSH/TLS).
Integrity monitoring: AIDE/Tripwire for file changes, Secure Boot + TPM for boot chain verification.

questions.question: Compare DAC, MAC, and RBAC access control models in the context of cloud OS security.
keyPoints: DAC (Discretionary): Traditional Unix permissions — owner controls access. Flexible but error-prone.
MAC (Mandatory): System-enforced policies that override DAC. SELinux and AppArmor implement MAC. Preferred for high-security cloud hosts.
RBAC (Role-Based): Access based on roles rather than individuals. Common in cloud management consoles.
Cloud environments typically use MAC on the Host OS (SELinux) and RBAC for cloud service access management.

questions.question: Discuss the OS-level vulnerabilities that are unique to cloud computing environments.
keyPoints: Shared kernel in containers: all containers share the host kernel, so a kernel exploit affects all containers on the host.
Management OS exposure: Dom0 in Xen has direct hypervisor access — compromising it equals compromising the hypervisor.
Side-channel attacks: Spectre and Meltdown exploit shared CPU behavior to leak data across VM boundaries.
Privilege escalation on Host OS is more severe than on physical servers because it affects all tenants.
Insider threats: cloud administrators with Host OS access can access all VM data, requiring strong audit controls.
